subtopic_detail: Atrial flutter haemodynamically stable rate control
stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and reflux presents to ED with 6 hours of palpitations and mild exertional dyspnoea. He has no chest pain, no syncope, no heart failure symptoms and a known LV ejection fraction of 58 percent on a recent transthoracic echocardiogram. His CHA2DS2-VA score is 3. He is otherwise well, has no chronic kidney disease and weighs 84 kg. There is no obvious precipitant such as alcohol or recent infection.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86, HR 138 irregularly regular pattern on monitor, RR 18, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Clear lungs, no peripheral oedema, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: ECG shows sawtooth flutter waves with 2:1 AV conduction, ventricular rate 138; troponin negative; TSH 1.4 mU/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L; potassium 4.1 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial rate-control choice?

options.text: Oral metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily commenced now with cardiology follow-up for outpatient anticoagulation decision after CHA2DS2-VA review
options.rationale: Beta blockade is first-line rate control in atrial flutter with preserved LVEF and no heart failure; CHA2DS2-VA above 2 requires anticoagulation review. (Source: NHFA atrial fibrillation guideline; CSANZ)

options.text: Intravenous adenosine 6 mg rapid push then 12 mg as the first-line agent to convert flutter rhythm to sinus rhythm in the emergency department
options.rationale: Adenosine transiently unmasks flutter waves but does not convert flutter; it is diagnostic, not therapeutic, in this rhythm. (Source: NHFA atrial fibrillation guideline; CSANZ)

options.text: Intravenous amiodarone 300 mg over 1 hour then 900 mg over 24 hours as the first-line agent for rate control in stable atrial flutter
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reserved for rhythm control in selected patients, often with heart failure; it is not first-line rate control for stable preserved-EF flutter. (Source: NHFA atrial fibrillation guideline; CSANZ)

options.text: Immediate synchronised DC cardioversion under sedation despite haemodynamic stability and a duration of arrhythmia under 12 hours without anticoagulation
options.rationale: Elective cardioversion in stable patients with arrhythmia under 48 hours can be considered but is not the routine first step; rate control with anticoagulation review is the AU pathway. (Source: NHFA atrial fibrillation guideline; CSANZ)

options.text: Oral digoxin 250 micrograms daily as monotherapy for rate control in this active 72-year-old with preserved left ventricular ejection fraction and reflux
options.rationale: Digoxin monotherapy provides poor rate control during exertion and is reserved for patients with sedentary lifestyle or as add-on; not first-line in this man. (Source: NHFA atrial fibrillation guideline; CSANZ)

explanation.summary: Stable atrial flutter with preserved LVEF is rate-controlled with a beta blocker (or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker if beta blockade is not tolerated). Digoxin is reserved for sedentary patients or as an add-on. Adenosine has a diagnostic role by transiently unmasking flutter waves but does not convert. Anticoagulation follows CHA2DS2-VA assessment; this man with a score of 3 warrants long-term oral anticoagulation.
key_points: Beta blockers are first-line rate control in flutter with preserved LVEF
Adenosine is diagnostic, not therapeutic, in flutter
CHA2DS2-VA >= 2 prompts oral anticoagulation in Australian guidance
explanation.pearls: A regularly conducted narrow-complex tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm is flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise; vagal manoeuvres or adenosine will reveal the sawtooth pattern.

subtopic_detail: GBS-positive women in labour at term
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 at 39+4 weeks arrives in spontaneous early labour with regular contractions every 5 minutes for 2 hours. Her low-vaginal and rectal swab at 36 weeks returned positive for Group B Streptococcus. She has no penicillin allergy, no history of GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy and no preterm rupture of membranes. The fetus is cephalic with a reassuring CTG. The cervix is 3 cm dilated and 1 cm long.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 122/74, HR 88, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm dilated, fully effaced; membranes intact
data_table.Investigations: Booking bloods unremarkable; GBS swab at 36+2 weeks positive on culture; no prior infant with invasive GBS disease

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate intrapartum management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics entirely until rupture of membranes occurs, then commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading and 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth
options.rationale: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is started in active labour, not delayed until ROM, to ensure two doses are completed before birth where possible. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours throughout labour with monitoring for anaphylaxis and signs of chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: AU first-line intrapartum GBS prophylaxis in a non-allergic woman is IV benzylpenicillin: 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours as a broad-spectrum substitute since the GBS may be penicillin-tolerant on culture
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin resistance; it is not preferred in non-allergic women. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Offer oral amoxicillin 1 g eight hourly throughout labour because oral absorption is rapid enough to achieve protective neonatal blood levels at delivery
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve reliable amniotic fluid levels in labour; IV therapy is required. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Recommend immediate caesarean section to avoid vaginal transit and eliminate the need for any intrapartum antimicrobial prophylaxis whatsoever today
options.rationale: GBS colonisation alone is not an indication for caesarean; AU practice is intrapartum IV prophylaxis with planned vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin (3 g load, 1.8 g every 4 hours) is the AU regimen for GBS-colonised women in labour to prevent early-onset neonatal disease. Two or more doses before delivery achieve adequate fetal serum levels. Severe penicillin allergy prompts clindamycin if susceptibilities permit, or vancomycin otherwise. GBS positivity is not an indication for caesarean. The cefazolin alternative is used for non-severe penicillin allergy.
key_points: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g then 1.8 g every 4 hours is the AU intrapartum GBS regimen
Two or more doses before birth achieve adequate fetal serum levels
Severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin resistance prompts vancomycin
explanation.pearls: Two doses of intrapartum benzylpenicillin given at least 4 hours apart before delivery achieves around 95 percent reduction in early-onset neonatal GBS disease.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: prevention of early-onset neonatal GBS

subtopic_detail: Urinary tract infection in first trimester
stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 11+2 weeks attends her GP with 2 days of urinary frequency, dysuria and suprapubic discomfort. She has no fever, no flank pain and no vaginal discharge. She has no penicillin allergy and no history of antibiotic-resistant infections. She is otherwise well, takes folic acid and iodine, and is afebrile in clinic. Her last menstrual period was 11 weeks and 2 days ago with a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy at the dating scan.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, BP 110/68, HR 76, RR 14
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness, no costovertebral angle tenderness, no abdominal guarding
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: leukocytes 3+, nitrites positive, protein trace, blood negative; midstream urine sent for culture; HbA1c 5.0% at booking

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic choice?

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days as the first-line agent for uncomplicated lower UTI in the first trimester of pregnancy in this woman
options.rationale: Trimethoprim is a folate antagonist and is avoided in the first trimester because of neural tube defect risk. (Source: AMH: trimethoprim in pregnancy)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg four times daily for 5 days as the first-line agent for first-trimester urinary tract infection in this otherwise well woman
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin is acceptable in mid-pregnancy but generally avoided near term (G6PD haemolysis risk in neonate); cephalexin is preferred for first-trimester empirical use. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: UTI in pregnancy)

options.text: Oral cephalexin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days commenced today, with the midstream urine culture follow-up and rescript adjustment if resistance is identified
options.rationale: Cephalexin is the AU first-line empirical agent for lower UTI in pregnancy across all trimesters in a non-allergic woman. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: UTI in pregnancy)

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days as a broad-spectrum option for empirical treatment of uncomplicated lower UTI in a pregnant patient today
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones are avoided in pregnancy because of fetal cartilage toxicity in animal data and equivalence of safer agents in humans. (Source: AMH: ciprofloxacin in pregnancy)

options.text: Symptomatic management with oral fluids and urinary alkaliniser only, with a planned review and dipstick check in 48 hours at the GP practice
options.rationale: Symptomatic UTI in pregnancy must be treated to reduce pyelonephritis and preterm birth risk; symptomatic-only management is inadequate. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: UTI in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Lower UTI in pregnancy is treated empirically to reduce pyelonephritis and preterm birth risk. Cephalexin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days is the AU first-line option across all trimesters, with culture-guided adjustment. Trimethoprim is avoided in the first trimester (folate antagonism, NTD risk) and nitrofurantoin is generally avoided at term (neonatal haemolysis risk). Fluoroquinolones are avoided throughout pregnancy.
key_points: Cephalexin is AU first-line empirical therapy for lower UTI in pregnancy
Trimethoprim is avoided in the first trimester because of NTD risk
Nitrofurantoin is avoided near term to minimise neonatal haemolysis risk
explanation.pearls: Always send a midstream urine culture in pregnancy UTI before starting empirical cephalexin; rescript on culture results within 48 hours if resistance is identified.

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding refractory to LNG-IUS
stem: A 44-year-old multiparous woman has had a levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 9 months ago for heavy menstrual bleeding with intramural fibroids up to 4 cm. She continues to bleed heavily for 7 days each cycle with daily flooding, and her ferritin has dropped to 12 micrograms/L despite oral iron supplementation. She has completed her family and is keen to avoid hysterectomy if a stepwise medical option remains. The Mirena is correctly sited on transvaginal ultrasound.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable mass; cervical os closed, IUD strings visible at expected length
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L (was 124 g/L 1 year ago), ferritin 12 micrograms/L, TSH 1.6 mU/L, coagulation profile normal; TVS shows three intramural fibroids 2 to 4 cm, endometrial thickness 6 mm, LNG-IUS in situ correctly

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next medical option?

options.text: Refer urgently for total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingectomy as the only definitive option given her age, parity and family completion at this point
options.rationale: Surgical options remain valid but the question asks about a stepwise medical option before considering hysterectomy. (Source: RANZCOG heavy menstrual bleeding statement)

options.text: Switch to a depot medroxyprogesterone acetate injection every 12 weeks alongside the existing intrauterine device for additive progestogenic suppression of the endometrium
options.rationale: Stacking progestogens does not reliably reduce bleeding further and adds bone density and adherence concerns; not a first-step option here. (Source: RANZCOG heavy menstrual bleeding statement)

options.text: Commence oral combined oral contraceptive cyclically with the LNG-IUS in situ to add oestrogen-mediated endometrial stabilisation across her remaining cycles
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptives may be considered but the LNG-IUS plus tranexamic acid combination is the simpler evidence-based next step for refractory bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG heavy menstrual bleeding statement)

options.text: Add oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during the heaviest 4 days of menstrual flow each cycle plus oral iron, with surgical review if no improvement in 3 months
options.rationale: Adding cyclical tranexamic acid to a correctly sited LNG-IUS reduces measured menstrual blood loss by an additional 30 to 50 percent and is appropriate before surgical referral. (Source: RANZCOG heavy menstrual bleeding statement)

options.text: Commence a GnRH agonist as long-term monotherapy without add-back hormonal therapy for ongoing suppression of fibroid-associated bleeding in this woman over the next 12 months
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are short-term bridges (3 to 6 months) before surgery and require add-back HRT after 6 months to limit bone loss. (Source: RANZCOG heavy menstrual bleeding statement)

explanation.summary: When heavy menstrual bleeding persists despite a correctly sited LNG-IUS, the next step is to add tranexamic acid (1 g three times daily during heavy bleeding days) and treat iron deficiency. NSAIDs can also be added cyclically. If bleeding still fails to settle, second-line options include endometrial ablation, uterine artery embolisation (for fibroids), myomectomy or hysterectomy depending on fertility wishes. GnRH agonists are short-term pre-operative bridges.
key_points: Cyclical tranexamic acid adds about 30 to 50 percent further reduction in menstrual blood loss on top of an LNG-IUS
GnRH agonists are short-term bridges (3 to 6 months) before surgery, with add-back HRT after 6 months
Treat iron deficiency aggressively alongside any haemorrhage-control measure
explanation.pearls: Cyclical tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during the heaviest 4 days reduces measured menstrual blood loss by around 40 percent and is taken only on bleeding days.

sources.label: AMH: tranexamic acid for heavy menstrual bleeding

stem: A 54-year-old woman presents to ED with 8 hours of severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, with vomiting. She has no fever, no jaundice and no haemodynamic instability. Her lipase is 8 times the upper limit of normal and her ALT is twice normal. An abdominal ultrasound shows gallstones in a non-distended gallbladder, a normal-calibre common bile duct without stones, and no choledocholithiasis. She is haemodynamically stable and tolerates small sips of water in ED.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 37.2 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness without peritonism, no guarding; bowel sounds normal; no Cullen or Grey Turner signs
data_table.Investigations: Lipase 1880 U/L, ALT 92 U/L, bilirubin 18 micromol/L, GGT 124 U/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, calcium 2.32 mmol/L, urea 5.2 mmol/L, CRP 24 mg/L; ultrasound as described above

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial in-hospital management plan?

options.text: Urgent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography within 24 hours regardless of jaundice status or imaging evidence of choledocholithiasis identified
options.rationale: Urgent ERCP is reserved for gallstone pancreatitis with ascending cholangitis or persisting obstruction; without those features there is no early ERCP benefit. (Source: GESA gallstone disease guidance)

options.text: Admit, restrict to nil by mouth for 5 to 7 days, total parenteral nutrition from day 1 and re-image with CT to grade severity at 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Prolonged NBM and TPN are no longer preferred; early enteral feeding within 24 to 48 hours improves outcomes; routine early CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty or deterioration. (Source: GESA gallstone disease guidance)

options.text: Discharge home with oral analgesia and elective outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks regardless of ongoing pain, hydration status or oral tolerance during the admission today
options.rationale: Acute pancreatitis requires admission for IV fluids and analgesia; discharge from ED with active disease is inappropriate. (Source: GESA gallstone disease guidance)

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g six hourly from admission to prevent peripancreatic infection in this patient with mild gallstone pancreatitis today
options.rationale: Prophylactic antibiotics do not improve outcomes in mild pancreatitis and are reserved for proven infection or infected necrosis. (Source: GESA gallstone disease guidance)

options.text: Admit for intravenous Hartmann's resuscitation, parenteral analgesia, early oral or enteral feeding within 24 to 48 hours and same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy
options.rationale: AU and GESA guidance recommend goal-directed fluids, early feeding and same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis. (Source: GESA gallstone disease guidance)

explanation.summary: Mild gallstone pancreatitis is managed with goal-directed intravenous crystalloid (Hartmann's preferred), parenteral analgesia, early oral or enteral feeding within 24 to 48 hours, and same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence. Prophylactic antibiotics do not improve outcomes. Urgent ERCP is reserved for cholangitis or persisting common bile duct obstruction. Severity assessment can be made with bedside scores supplemented by lactate and urea trends.
key_points: Same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy is recommended for mild gallstone pancreatitis
Early enteral feeding within 24 to 48 hours improves outcomes versus prolonged NBM
Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated in mild pancreatitis without infection
explanation.pearls: Same-admission cholecystectomy after mild gallstone pancreatitis reduces 30-day readmission for recurrent biliary events by around 75 percent compared with delayed surgery.
